questions.stem: Herd immunity refers to
options: the total number of infectives at the peak
the fraction of the population that must be immune so that the disease cannot spread
the rate at which infectives recover
questions.explanation: Herd immunity is the proportion of the population (the susceptibles) that must be made immune, usually by vaccination, in order to stop the disease from spreading. It is a threshold fraction, not a count of infectives, a recovery rate, or a latent period. Reaching it pushes the effective reproduction number below one.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: The herd immunity threshold, the minimum fraction $P$ that must be immunized to prevent an epidemic, is
questions.explanation: Immunizing a fraction $P$ replaces $S_0$ by $S_0(1 - P)$ in the reproduction number, and requiring the result below one gives $\frac{\beta S_0 (1 - P)}{\gamma} < 1$, hence $P > 1 - \frac{1}{R_0}$. The other forms misplace the reciprocal or the subtraction. The critical immunization fraction is $1 - \frac{1}{R_0}$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Immunizing a fraction $P$ of the population has what effect in the SIR model?
options: It increases the susceptibles to $S_0(1 + P)$
It replaces the initial susceptibles $S_0$ by $S_0(1 - P)$, lowering $R_0$
questions.explanation: Vaccination transfers individuals from the susceptible class to the immune (removed) class, so the effective initial susceptibles become $S_0(1 - P)$. Since $R_0 = \frac{\beta S_0}{\gamma}$, this reduces the reproduction number to $\frac{\beta S_0 (1 - P)}{\gamma}$. It does not raise $\beta$, increase susceptibles, or leave $R_0$ untouched. Fewer susceptibles means a smaller reproduction number.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Starting from the requirement $\dfrac{\beta S_0 (1 - P)}{\gamma} < 1$, the herd immunity condition is derived as
questions.explanation: Since $R_0 = \frac{\beta S_0}{\gamma}$, the inequality $\frac{\beta S_0 (1 - P)}{\gamma} < 1$ becomes $R_0(1 - P) < 1$, so $1 - P < \frac{1}{R_0}$, giving $P > 1 - \frac{1}{R_0}$. The direction of the inequality and the placement of $P$ in the other options are wrong. The threshold is $P > 1 - \frac{1}{R_0}$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: For a disease with $R_0 = 4$, the minimum fraction that must be immunized to prevent an epidemic is
questions.explanation: The threshold is $P > 1 - \frac{1}{R_0} = 1 - \frac{1}{4} = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$. So at least $75\%$ of the population must be immune. Reporting $0.25$ gives $\frac{1}{R_0}$, the fraction that may remain susceptible, not the fraction to immunize. The herd immunity threshold is $0.75$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: A disease has $R_0 = 5$. The herd immunity threshold is
questions.explanation: Compute $1 - \frac{1}{R_0} = 1 - \frac{1}{5} = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8$, that is $80\%$. So $80\%$ of the population must be immune to block sustained transmission. The value $20\%$ is $\frac{1}{R_0}$, the maximum that may stay susceptible. The herd immunity threshold is $80\%$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: A disease has $R_0 = 2$. What fraction of the population must be immunized to prevent an epidemic?
questions.explanation: Here $1 - \frac{1}{R_0} = 1 - \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$, so at least half the population must be immune. A reproduction number of $2$ is comparatively low, so the required coverage is moderate. The other values misuse the formula. The threshold is $0.5$, or $50\%$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: A disease has $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3000}$ per day, an average infectious period of $6$ days, in a town of $1200$ people. Using $R_0 = \dfrac{\beta N}{\gamma}$, the reproduction number is
questions.explanation: The recovery rate is $\gamma = \frac{1}{6}$ per day. Then $R_0 = \frac{\beta N}{\gamma} = \frac{(1/3000)(1200)}{1/6} = \frac{0.4}{1/6} = 0.4 \times 6 = 2.4$. So each case produces $2.4$ secondary infections on average. The intermediate value $0.4$ is $\beta N$ before dividing by $\gamma$. The reproduction number is $2.4$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: For that town (population $1200$, $R_0 = 2.4$), how many inhabitants must be immunized to avoid an epidemic?
questions.explanation: The threshold fraction is $1 - \frac{1}{R_0} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.4} = 1 - 0.41\overline{6} = 0.58\overline{3} = \frac{7}{12}$. Multiplying by the population, $\frac{7}{12} \times 1200 = 700$. So $700$ inhabitants must be immunized. The complement $500$ is the number that may remain susceptible. The required number is $700$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: When a fraction $P$ is vaccinated, the SIR susceptible equation becomes
questions.explanation: Vaccinating a fraction $P$ leaves only $(1 - P)$ of the contacts able to lead to new infection, so the effective incidence is scaled by $(1 - P)$, giving $\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta(1 - P) S I$. The sign stays negative because susceptibles are lost to infection. Using $1 + P$, only $P$, or a positive sign would all be incorrect. The factor is $(1 - P)$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Why does a higher $R_0$ require a higher vaccination coverage to achieve herd immunity?
options: Because vaccination stops working at high $R_0$
Because the formula does not depend on $R_0$
Because higher $R_0$ lowers the required coverage
questions.explanation: As $R_0$ increases, $\frac{1}{R_0}$ shrinks, so the threshold $1 - \frac{1}{R_0}$ rises toward $1$. A more transmissible disease therefore demands that a larger share of the population be immune to interrupt spread. The coverage clearly depends on $R_0$ and increases with it, and vaccination does not stop working. Greater transmissibility means a higher coverage requirement.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Measles has a very high $R_0 \approx 15$. The approximate herd immunity threshold is
questions.explanation: Compute $1 - \frac{1}{15} = 1 - 0.0667 \approx 0.933$, roughly $93\%$. Such a high threshold is why measles requires very high vaccination coverage to control. The value $7\%$ is $\frac{1}{R_0}$, the small fraction that may remain susceptible. The herd immunity threshold is about $93\%$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: If a disease has $R_0 = 1.25$, the herd immunity threshold is
questions.explanation: Compute $1 - \frac{1}{1.25} = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2$, so only $20\%$ coverage is needed for a mildly transmissible disease. The value $0.8$ is $\frac{1}{R_0}$, the fraction that may stay susceptible. The threshold is $0.2$, or $20\%$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: A town of $1200$ people faces a disease with $R_0 = 3$. How many people must be immunized to reach herd immunity?
questions.explanation: The threshold fraction is $1 - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$. Multiplying by the population, $\frac{2}{3} \times 1200 = 800$. So $800$ inhabitants must be immunized, leaving $400$ who may remain susceptible. The required number is $800$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: The effective reproduction number after immunizing a fraction $P$ is $R_0(1 - P)$. Vaccinating $P = 0.6$ when $R_0 = 4$ gives an effective value of
questions.explanation: Compute $R_0(1 - P) = 4 \times (1 - 0.6) = 4 \times 0.4 = 1.6$. Since this effective value $1.6$ still exceeds one, $60\%$ coverage is not enough and the disease can still spread. To stop it the coverage would need to exceed $1 - \frac{1}{4} = 0.75$. The effective reproduction number is $1.6$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Continuing with $R_0 = 4$, is vaccinating $60\%$ of the population enough to prevent an epidemic?
options: No, because the effective reproduction number $1.6$ is still above one
Yes, because $60\%$ exceeds half the population
Yes, because any vaccination prevents epidemics
questions.explanation: With $60\%$ coverage the effective reproduction number is $R_0(1 - P) = 4 \times 0.4 = 1.6 > 1$, so transmission still sustains and an epidemic can occur. The required threshold is $1 - \frac{1}{4} = 75\%$, above $60\%$. Exceeding half is not the criterion, partial vaccination does not guarantee prevention, and vaccination lowers rather than raises $R_0$. Sixty percent is insufficient here.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: The purpose of reducing $R_0$ below one through immunization is to
options: ensure each infective produces fewer than one new case, so the disease cannot establish
questions.explanation: Pushing the effective reproduction number below one means each infective on average causes fewer than one secondary case, so any introduced infection dies out rather than growing into an epidemic. Immunization achieves this by removing susceptibles. It does not increase susceptibles, lengthen infectiousness, or raise transmission. The aim is to break the chain of transmission.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Herd immunity protects unvaccinated individuals because
options: the disease mutates to become harmless
the vaccine is transferred directly to them
enough surrounding people are immune that transmission chains to the unvaccinated rarely complete
the unvaccinated cannot contract the disease
questions.explanation: When the immune fraction exceeds the threshold, an infective is mostly surrounded by immune contacts, so chains of transmission seldom reach the remaining susceptibles, indirectly protecting those not vaccinated. The protection is not by transferring vaccine, by the pathogen becoming harmless, or by making the unvaccinated inherently immune. It is the scarcity of viable transmission paths.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: If $90\%$ of a population is immune to a disease with $R_0 = 5$, what is the effective reproduction number, and will the disease spread?
questions.explanation: The effective reproduction number is $R_0(1 - P) = 5 \times (1 - 0.9) = 5 \times 0.1 = 0.5$. Since $0.5 < 1$, the disease cannot sustain transmission and will not spread. The threshold here is $1 - \frac{1}{5} = 0.8$, and $90\%$ comfortably exceeds it. The effective value is $0.5$ and the disease dies out.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Two diseases circulate: disease X has $R_0 = 2$ and disease Y has $R_0 = 10$. Which needs higher vaccination coverage for herd immunity?
options: Disease Y, requiring $90\%$ versus $50\%$ for X
Disease X, requiring $90\%$ versus $50\%$ for Y
questions.explanation: For X, $1 - \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$ ($50\%$); for Y, $1 - \frac{1}{10} = 0.9$ ($90\%$). The more transmissible disease Y needs the higher coverage. Coverage rises with $R_0$, so the requirements differ and are not equal, and both diseases have $R_0 > 1$ so both need vaccination to reach herd immunity. Disease Y demands $90\%$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: A disease has $R_0 = 8$. The maximum fraction of the population that may remain susceptible without an epidemic is
questions.explanation: Herd immunity requires immunizing more than $1 - \frac{1}{R_0}$, so the largest susceptible fraction that still prevents spread is $\frac{1}{R_0} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$. The complement $\frac{7}{8}$ is the fraction that must be immune. The maximum susceptible fraction is $0.125$, or $12.5\%$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Apply the herd immunity formula for a given reproduction number.
questions.solution: A disease has $R_0 = 4$. The minimum fraction that must be immunized to prevent an epidemic is $P = 1 - 1/R_0 =$ {{1}}. The largest fraction that may stay susceptible is $1/R_0 =$ {{2}}. Because the required coverage is high, the disease is {{3}} transmissible than one with $R_0 = 1.5$.
questions.explanation: The threshold is $1 - \frac{1}{4} = 0.75$, so $75\%$ must be immune, while at most $\frac{1}{4} = 0.25$ may remain susceptible. A higher required coverage corresponds to a more transmissible disease than one with $R_0 = 1.5$ (whose threshold is only about $0.33$).
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Work the classic immunization example from reproduction number to head count.
questions.solution: In a town of $1200$ people a disease has $\beta = 1/3000$ per day and an average infectious period of $6$ days, so $\gamma = 1/6$ per day. Then $R_0 = \beta N/\gamma =$ {{1}}. The herd immunity threshold is the fraction $7/12$, so the number of inhabitants who must be immunized is {{2}}. The number who may remain susceptible is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $R_0 = \frac{(1/3000)(1200)}{1/6} = 0.4 \times 6 = 2.4$. The threshold is $1 - \frac{1}{2.4} = \frac{7}{12}$, so the number immunized is $\frac{7}{12} \times 1200 = 700$, leaving $1200 - 700 = 500$ susceptible.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Derive the herd immunity condition from the effective reproduction number.
questions.solution: Immunizing a fraction $P$ replaces the susceptibles $S_0$ by $S_0($ {{1}} $)$, so the reproduction number becomes $R_0(1 - P)$. Requiring this to be below {{2}} and solving gives the condition $P > 1 - 1/R_0$. The sign in front of $1/R_0$ in this threshold is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Vaccination scales susceptibles to $S_0(1 - P)$, giving effective reproduction number $R_0(1 - P)$. Setting $R_0(1 - P) < 1$ and rearranging yields $P > 1 - \frac{1}{R_0}$, in which the $\frac{1}{R_0}$ term carries a negative sign.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Test whether a proposed coverage level stops an outbreak.
questions.solution: A disease has $R_0 = 5$ and a campaign vaccinates $70\%$ of the population. The effective reproduction number is $R_0(1 - P) =$ {{1}}. Since this value is {{2}} one, the campaign {{3}} prevent the epidemic, because the required threshold is $1 - 1/5 = 0.8$.
questions.explanation: The effective reproduction number is $5 \times (1 - 0.7) = 5 \times 0.3 = 1.5 > 1$, so transmission still sustains and the campaign does not prevent the epidemic. The needed coverage is $1 - \frac{1}{5} = 0.8$, above the $0.7$ achieved.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Compare coverage requirements across diseases of differing transmissibility.
questions.solution: Disease A has $R_0 = 2$ and disease B has $R_0 = 10$. The herd immunity threshold for A is {{1}} and for B is {{2}}. Therefore the disease that is harder to control through vaccination is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: For A, $1 - \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$; for B, $1 - \frac{1}{10} = 0.9$. The higher threshold for disease B means it needs more coverage and is harder to control through vaccination.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology
